glossary.def: The exact splitting of expected squared-error test loss into bias² (how far the average model is from the truth), variance (how much it jitters as the training set is resampled), and irreducible noise σ². It is the lens that explains every ensemble method: bagging shrinks variance, boosting shrinks bias, and no method can beat the noise floor.

Breiman's method of fitting one high-variance, low-bias learner on each of k bootstrap resamples of the data and averaging (or voting) their predictions. It reduces the variance term toward the correlation floor ρσ² while leaving bias essentially unchanged.

A validation estimate that comes free with bagging: since each bootstrap sample omits about 36.8% (e⁻¹) of the points, averaging each point's prediction over only the trees that did not see it gives an unbiased error estimate with no separate holdout set.

Bagging plus per-split feature subsampling (√p features for classification, p/3 for regression). The extra randomization decorrelates the trees — driving ρ in the correlated-average variance formula lower than plain bagging can — at a small cost in individual-tree accuracy.

An ensemble built sequentially, where each new weak learner is trained to correct the running ensemble's mistakes. Unlike bagging, it attacks the bias term by composing many high-bias weak models into one strong one; its members are correlated by construction, so it forgoes variance reduction.

Friedman's view of boosting as gradient descent in function space: each round fits a weak learner to the negative gradient of the loss (the residual, for squared error) and adds a shrunken step F_m = F_{m-1} + ν·h_m. Small learning rate ν with many rounds regularizes the fit, the same logic as small SGD steps.

The original adaptive-reweighting boosting algorithm: misclassified points are up-weighted so the next weak learner focuses on them, and each learner gets a vote α = log((1−err)/err) + log(K−1). SAMME is the multiclass form; for K=2 it reduces to classic AdaBoost. It is sensitive to label noise because its exponential loss punishes outliers harshly.

Wolpert's method of training a second-level meta-learner whose inputs are the predictions of several diverse base models. The base predictions must be out-of-fold (cross-validated) to avoid leakage; a simple regularized meta-model (e.g. ridge) then learns, per region, which base model to trust.

refs.cite: Breiman, L. (1996). Bagging Predictors. Machine Learning 24(2).
Breiman, L. (2001). Random Forests. Machine Learning 45(1).
Wolpert, D. H. (1992). Stacked Generalization. Neural Networks 5(2).
Dietterich, T. G. (2000). Ensemble Methods in Machine Learning. Multiple Classifier Systems, LNCS 1857.
Freund, Y. & Schapire, R. E. (1997). A Decision-Theoretic Generalization of On-Line Learning and an Application to Boosting. Journal of Computer and System Sciences 55(1).
Friedman, J. H. (2001). Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine. Annals of Statistics 29(5).
Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R. & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.). Springer.
